comments.comment_body: Abhinav Parameshwaran
comments.post_body:
Google India is hiring Software Engineering (SDE) Interns for Summer 2026! 🚀

This is a fantastic opportunity, and you can apply directly here: https://lnkd.in/gzPuagK7

Don't make the mistake I did.

When I was a student, I didn't do any internships in Big Tech.

 Internship positions were available, but I didn't feel prepared enough to apply. 

By the time I did feel ready and started applying, most of the roles had already been filled. Needless to say, I was devastated.

Learn from my experience: Apply ASAP!

You'll have plenty of time to prepare for interviews between submitting your application and actually interviewing. The most crucial step is getting your application in early, ensuring you actually get a chance to interview.
comments.context_summary: The commenter is responding to a post about Google India hiring software engineering interns for Summer 2026, sharing their personal experience of missing out on Big Tech internships as a student and advising others to apply ASAP to avoid the same mistake. They emphasize the importance of getting applications in early to secure an interview opportunity.

comments.post_body:
Happy New Year to all my LinkedIn friends! 🥂 #2024

As we kick off 2024, I hope each of you had a chance to unwind and create memorable moments with your loved ones. 

For my candidates: apologies for the slight delay. An eye infection has turned my world a bit blurry, but I’m on it! Perhaps this year is about embracing a more measured pace – let’s see where that takes us.

Now, let's recap the exciting opportunities I'm eager to share with you all: 

Our #FullStack teams are expanding, and we have fantastic new opportunities for YOU!

Required skills: 
😎 Cloud technologies/public cloud. Tech agnostic (any #OOP) with a strong preference for expertise in Java, JavaScript, HTML, CSS, and SQL. 💰 Bonus: Big data, AI, ML, data visualization, experience with content management systems  
😎 Experience Levels: must have 4+ years of professional software development experience (Enterprise level) 
😎 Location: San Francisco, Seattle, or Bellevue only (hybrid) 
😎 Full Stack - Backend focused (80/20), 50/50 Full Stack, Frontend focused (80/20) 

🔥 Update: In order to help me review your resumes more efficiently, please send your updated resume to julia.li@salesforce.com. I will reach out to you if we may have an opening for you! Due to the volume, I may not be able to respond to all of your inquiries/emails. Thank you for your understanding! 

Looking forward to seeing your applications and connecting with the best and brightest in the industry – with clearer vision, very soon 😅 !

comments.comment_body: Hi Chip Turner im interested in software engineer opportunities!
comments.post_body:
I love working on AI at Meta and you should come join me.

A couple of months ago I returned to Meta to work on AI Infrastructure as part of the PyTorch team.  The energy, excitement, and enthusiasm inside Meta around AI today is beyond almost anything I saw in the first decade I was here.  It's a special time for us, and I couldn't be happier being back.

The scale of AI at Meta is staggering, and the innovation and ambition of our product teams and researchers is incredible to witness first-hand.  Every day I learn something new from the top experts in the field.

Meta's strategy around AI -- safety first with a deep commitment to Open Source -- aligns deeply with my own values.  Unlike the other large AI players, Meta has continued pushing open source and publishing innovative research.  And while Meta has a long history of prominent Open Source contributions (React, React Native, Zstandard, the Linux Kernel, ...), one in particular stands out in the field of AI: PyTorch. 

PyTorch, created by Meta, is the dominant deep learning framework throughout academia and industry.  Practically everything you see or hear related to AI is written in PyTorch as are most published research papers.  Our work here is powering the entire industry right now.

In addition to PyTorch, Meta has open sourced Llama 2, the largest and most compelling open source large language model.  You can literally download it and try it yourself and get a glimpse of how powerful and democratizing our strategy is.

I take great pride in our steadfast commitment to safe, responsible, and open AI innovation.  To be working alongside the teams of this caliber tackling the largest challenges in our field is something you can't do anywhere else.

If any of this sounds interesting to you, we have a wide range of openings for experienced candidates.  

EDIT: Wow this blew up!  Thank you everyone for commenting, connecting, liking, and reposting.  I will try my best to look through the reachouts I've received but please also check for specific roles on the Meta Careers page (https://lnkd.in/g7MPG_6T), particularly filtering both for Infrastructure and AI.
comments.context_summary: The commenter is responding to a post about working on AI at Meta, where the author expresses their enthusiasm for the company's AI infrastructure, innovation, and commitment to open source, and invites others to join the team. The post highlights Meta's work on PyTorch and Llama 2, and the commenter is likely responding to the author's invitation or sharing their own thoughts on Meta's AI efforts.

comments.comment_body: Interested in IC3/4 roles!
comments.post_body:
We are hiring for several roles at Meta #hiring

There are several exciting immediate openings (IC3/4/5/6/7) available in my org right now. 

What we do:
We're building the best video-calling experience across Family of Apps (e.g. Messenger, Instagram), with plenty of exciting opportunities to work on the cutting-edge technologies that connect billions of people together everyday. If working on highly distributed, scalable services in highly resource-constrained devices sound exciting to you, we want to hear from you!

If interested, please check the relevant sections:
For IC5+, please ping me directly with your resume so we can fast-track your application.
For IC4, please find roles in Careers page: https://lnkd.in/gftfAfr5
For IC3 (new grads), please apply here:  https://lnkd.in/g_f35Sv2 For new grad applications, please apply thru this link provided and leave a comment in the post for easy tracking. Thank you! 

The recruiting pipelines are different depending on the levels/experience, so going through the proper channels will greatly expedite the process and improve the outcomes. 

N.b: please only ping me if you have specific details to add to your application. This helps eliminate noise from my inbox and expedite the process. Thank you!
N.N.b: all applicants graduating within 2023+ (regardless of previous experience) will need to apply as new grad. Please check the link posted above for new grad applications. 

I’m #hiring at #Meta #AI #ML. Know anyone who might be interested?

comments.comment_body: I dont think you've answered my question - the how. I reframed my question, I think I may have not been clear. To your reply, why will fewer people apply?
comments.post_body:
735 applicants for 1 job. Or clicks.  (Calming down all who only look at the headline).

How could a company reasonably sort through all of these applicants? 

Consider this: 
Average resume is reviewed for 11 seconds 
~2% of applicants get an interview (14.7 of 735) 

Does this not seem rather inefficient? For all? 

If you’re interested in something different - job seekers and hiring managers - check out: 

CLEVERHIRE.co - wait list has been opened up. 

Launching this summer. 

#recruitmentsolutions #recruiting #jobs Eric Shedletsky Nikki Hawke Saad Uddin Sonia Carreno CleverHire
comments.context_summary: The post discusses the inefficiency of the traditional job application process, where 735 applicants are competing for one job, and highlights the brief time spent reviewing resumes and the low interview rate. The commenter is responding to this post, which also mentions an alternative recruitment solution called CLEVERHIRE.co.

If you’re interested in something different - job seekers and hiring managers - check out: 

CLEVERHIRE.co - wait list has been opened up. 

Launching this summer. 

#recruitmentsolutions #recruiting #jobs Eric Shedletsky Nikki Hawke Saad Uddin Sonia Carreno CleverHire

comments.comment_body: Because it’s more work. And that’s a good thing. People like “easy apply” which is why many of the commenters say unqualified people apply. Folks won’t take an extra 10 min to answer 1 way questions on video of they are not qualified . That’s our hypothesis and we have experience elsewhere that supports it. Thanks Anuj Narayanaswamy

comments.comment_body: Megan Lieu aside from the knowledge learned, is this certificate seen as any significant value to recruiters when put on resume?
comments.post_body:
Back in 2021 when I first began my pivot to data, there was only one online course I took:


Coursera’s Google Professional Certificate in Data Analytics has been and continues to be the only course I recommend to folks trying to break into data professions for a few reasons:

- It does a great job covering the technical fundamentals that I actually used everyday in my first data analytics role, such as spreadsheets, SQL, R and visualizations 

- The lessons go beyond technical concepts, covering how to actually land your first data analytics job—from networking, to resume writing to interviewing skills

- A capstone at the end of the course allows you to reinforce the concepts via project-based learning

Coursera currently has an offer where you can try the Data Analytics Certificate or any other Professional Certificate from Google for just $1 for your first month (normally $59). So if you’re an aspiring data analyst with no idea where to begin--run, don’t walk 👏

This is a limited-time offer and details on how to redeem it will be in my comments go go go 👇


#dataanalytics #coursera #careers
comments.context_summary: The commenter is recommending Coursera's Google Professional Certificate in Data Analytics for those trying to break into data professions, citing its comprehensive coverage of technical fundamentals and job landing skills. They also mention a limited-time offer to try the course for $1 for the first month.

comments.comment_body: Anuj Narayanaswamy I think it helps in allowing you to say you’ve done a real-world, hands-on project at the end of it. But probably not directly

comments.comment_body: Daliana Liu very insightful, thank you! Was wondering if you could share or know someone who could share a similar trend for software engineer roles?
comments.post_body:
Three data science interview trend in 2023:

1. Bad news: Interviews have become tougher to crack. 
There is a hiring freeze and fewer job openings.
The hiring standard is higher.
Top tech companies demand in-depth knowledge in A/B testing and SQL

2. Good news: people are still getting offers above $250k
Despite the economic downturn, there are still companies hiring.
She hasn't seem much low-ball offers. It's not uncommon to get offers above $250k, even above $300k.

3. Opportunity: companies value strong domain knowledge
For example: marketing, supply chain, etc. 
This means even if you only spent 50% time in your previous job in data science, if your knowledge in the industry matches the hiring team, you have an edge. 

I learned this from a chat with an interview coach. I'll share more about how to tackle data science interviews in 2023 and share resources in my next newsletter: www.dalianaliu.com

What do you want to know about 2023's data science interview trend?

#datascience
comments.context_summary: The post discusses three trends in data science interviews for 2023, including tougher hiring standards, high salary offers, and the value of strong domain knowledge, and the commenter is responding to the post by potentially seeking more information on these trends. The post is based on a conversation with an interview coach and invites readers to share their questions about data science interview trends.

comments.comment_body: Pratiksha Patnaik
comments.post_body:
It's time to head back to the office.

Google just sent an email out requesting workers back to the office 3 days a week.

It's part of everyone's performance review so there won't be an easy way around it.

Even if those employees can deliver their work.
Show up to every meeting.
Get things done.

Google wants their workers back.

It doesn't matter if there's personal obligations or if they're more productive at home.

It doesn't matter if their commute is 30+ minutes or if they need to work in the evening. 

Those workers will be 
• Badged
• Tracked
• Anxious

Even with 4 "work from anywhere weeks".

Nobody will tell you they only have personal obligations "4 weeks a year."

#alexcancode
#softwareengineer 
#coding

comments.comment_body: All very valid things! I resonate with all of them. For the last point 4 - I would recommend this - next time you are doing something you really love, hang back a second and let the happiness flow through you and you can tell yourself that you are really having a good time, I guess express some gratitude in the fact that you get satisfaction and happiness out of it. This I have found is a much better way to acknowledge happiness than to sit and ponder of the question "Am I happy for period of time?". In the course of time, eventually you want to make sure that you feel that happiness with as many things you do during the day - and of course there will be things that you don't like - but your acknowledgements might help your brain remember the good times also.
comments.post_body:
Over the last few years working as a software engineer, I became embarrassed mentioning these few things:

1. It gets lonely - Working as an adult taking care of home chores and other responsibilities doesn't give a lot of room to make friends. It hasn't been easy moving to another state for the sake of work.

2. I'm insecure about my level and compensation - Even working at Google doesn't make the feeling stop. I started comparing myself with new grad engineers with similar compensation and senior engineers who reached their level with less work experience.

3. Having a full-time job doesn't feel like enough - At 26, it feels like I should be doing more instead of spending my days as a software engineer at Google. There's others in YCombinator, making YT Videos, and starting non-profits.

4. I keep asking myself if I'm happy - I don't know if I want to climb the corporate ladder, spend more time with family, or invest in hobbies. There's pro's and con's to all of them and it's never been an easy decision to make.

These are some of the things I started thinking about more in my late 20's. 

#softwareengineer 
#alexcancode
#coding

comments.comment_body: Cfbr
comments.post_body:
Hi everyone,
My role at AWS has been eliminated. I recently started working full-time as an SDE at AWS after a successful summer internship.

While I have over 3+ years of full-time experience, Amazon was my first big tech opportunity. During my short tenure, I have seen tremendous professional growth. I owe all of this to my manager Chao Song and team members - Olga Voytovich, Shubhi Jain, Sukruti Puranam Hosudurg, Aaron Gupta, Matthew Crupi, Derek Wang. Through them, I was able to learn how to develop and maintain highly scalable web services. I also developed strong doc writing and communication skills.

I am now actively looking for new opportunities as a software engineer. I recently graduated from the University of Buffalo with an MS in CS and am currently in my first year of OPT. I have a limited number of days to secure employment. You can reach out to me at sumantra94@gmail.com. 

Thank you!

comments.comment_body: Is there any way to re-enter the h1b lottery for subsequent rounds through another employer for folks who have been laid off recently?
comments.post_body:
Late Breaking. 
WSJ Notes:
> 96,000 people submitted multiple lottery entries amounting to 408,000 entries.
> These multiple entries are coming from a "handful" of "small" companies that are now under in-depth investigation.

The number of H-1B applications for the lottery was inordinately high, with many multiple registrations. USCIS is making criminal referrals (for investigation and prosecution).

#USCIS #h1blottery
